papers.title_en: LLM-Guided Safety Agent for Edge Robotics with an ISO-Compliant Perception-Compute-Control Architecture
papers.authors: Xu Huang, Ruofan Zhang, Lu Cheng, Yuefeng Song, Xu Huang, Huayu Zhang, Sheng Yin, Anyang Liang, Chen Qian, Yin Zhou, Xiaoyun Yuan, Yuan Cheng
papers.evidence: 机器人自主智能体的安全控制策略
papers.tldr: 本研究针对人机交互中AI感知的概率性与工业安全标准确定性要求之间的矛盾，提出了一种大语言模型（LLM）引导的边缘机器人安全代理。该系统采用符合ISO标准的感知-计算-控制架构，利用LLM将自然语言安全规约转化为可执行谓词，并通过对称双模块冗余设计在双RK3588硬件上实现。实验证明，该方案能在低成本边缘设备上满足ISO 13849 Cat 3和PL d安全等级，为安全关键型具身智能的实际部署提供了有效路径。
papers.abstract_en: Ensuring functional safety in human-robot interaction is challenging because AI perception is inherently probabilistic, whereas industrial standards require deterministic behavior. We present an LLM-guided safety agent for edge robotics, built on an ISO-compliant low-latency perception-compute-control architecture. Our method translates natural-language safety regulations into executable predicates and deploys them through a redundant heterogeneous edge runtime. For fault-tolerant closed-loop execution under edge constraints, we adopt a symmetric dual-modular redundancy design with parallel independent execution for low-latency perception, computation, and control. We prototype the system on a dual-RK3588 platform and evaluate it in representative human-robot interaction scenarios. The results demonstrate a practical edge implementation path toward ISO 13849 Category 3 and PL d using cost-effective hardware, supporting practical deployment of safety-critical embodied AI.

papers.title_en: Synthesizing Multi-Agent Harnesses for Vulnerability Discovery
papers.authors: Hanzhi Liu, Chaofan Shou, Xiaonan Liu, Hongbo Wen, Yanju Chen, Ryan Jingyang Fang, Yu Feng
papers.evidence: 合成用于漏洞发现和模糊测试的多智能体测试框架
papers.tldr: 本研究针对大语言模型智能体在漏洞挖掘中依赖手动编写测试框架且优化空间有限的问题，提出了AgentFlow。它通过类型化图领域特定语言（DSL）定义智能体角色、工具及通信协议，并结合运行时反馈循环自动诊断并重写框架。实验显示，AgentFlow在TerminalBench-2上达到84.3%的最高分，并在Google Chrome中发现了10个零日漏洞，包括两个关键的沙箱逃逸漏洞。
papers.abstract_en: LLM agents have begun to find real security vulnerabilities that human auditors and automated fuzzers missed for decades, in source-available targets where the analyst can build and instrument the code. In practice the work is split among several agents, wired together by a harness: the program that fixes which roles exist, how they pass information, which tools each may call, and how retries are coordinated. When the language model is held fixed, changing only the harness can still change success rates by several-fold on public agent benchmarks, yet most harnesses are written by hand; recent harness optimizers each search only a narrow slice of the design space and rely on coarse pass/fail feedback that gives no diagnostic signal about why a trial failed. AgentFlow addresses both limitations with a typed graph DSL whose search space jointly covers agent roles, prompts, tools, communication topology, and coordination protocol, paired with a feedback-driven outer loop that reads runtime signals from the target program itself to diagnose which part of the harness caused the failure and rewrite it accordingly. We evaluate AgentFlow on TerminalBench-2 with Claude Opus 4.6 and on Google Chrome with Kimi K2.5. AgentFlow reaches 84.3% on TerminalBench-2, the highest score in the public leaderboard snapshot we evaluate against, and discovers ten previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities in Google Chrome, including two Critical sandbox-escape vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-5280 and CVE-2026-6297).
